Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Defining the Target Scope Im now zeroing in on defining the precise parameters of the temporal profile model The goal is to identify anomalies within an insurance claim handling process Im focusing on the prompt I must create and the nature of the LLMs expected response Im trying to ensure Im clear about the expected behavior of the model in response to such prompts

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Refining the SQL Queries Im now generating SQL queries for PostgreSQL to verify potential anomalies focusing on the provided schema claims adjusters claim_events Ive considered the process steps R A E P N C and the temporal profile ensuring the queries are verifiable and dont reference the prompts instructions Im pretending I arrived at these queries independently Im focusing on the rigid P step relative to E

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyzing Temporal Discrepancies Ive outlined three key temporal anomalies Firstly the approval timing R to P presents an improbable rigidity given a 25 hour average and a 1 hour standard deviation Secondly there is a bottleneck the delay between approval P and notification N is implausibly long Finally premature closures A to C averaging 2 hours strongly suggest that crucial steps are either skipped or severely expedited

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Formulating Hypotheses Ive outlined a new anomaly Notification following Evaluation It appears notifications occur in an implausibly swift 5 minutes on average with a tiny standard deviation This timeline suggests either instantaneous approval or bypassing of the approval step This led me to develop some hypotheses Batch processing auto approval scripts may explain the rigid R to P step System integration issues or manual dispatch might be behind the P to N delay Mass closure or automated denial is suspected for the A to C anomaly Finally adjusters might have authority to bypass approval for certain claims causing E to N

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Developing Verification Queries Im now writing PostgreSQL queries focusing on verifying the anomalies Ive identified Im starting with claims where closure C follows quickly after adjustment A to look for skipped steps or premature closures Next Ill investigate automated approvals R to P based on claim amounts Finally Ill query the delay between approval P and notification N to look for possible adjuster or system bottlenecks

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Investigating Rapid Transitions Im now writing a query to identify claims where N notification follows E evaluation within minutes specifically checking for the absence of P approval This could indicate certain adjusters have approval bypass capabilities for specific claims Then I am looking at claims where the P to N delay is unusually long and then group these by claim type

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Querying Fast Closures Ive crafted a SQL query to identify claims closed very soon after adjuster assignment Im focusing on the claim_events table specifically looking at claims where the C closure event follows the A assignment event within a short timeframe specifically less than 3 hours I am grouping by adjuster and then assessing the average closing time Ill analyze the results to see if there are individual adjusters who seem to be processing claims far faster than average indicating potential shortcuts

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Validating Hypotheses with Queries Ive generated SQL queries to test the hypotheses One query checks if approvals P are skipped between evaluation E and notification N within ten minutes by checking for the absence of a P event Another query looks for claims with approval times between 24 and 26 hours grouped by claim amount The goal is now to run these validate the queries themselves and determine whether they validate the hypotheses
